one_line_takeaway: Forging a model's own chat-template role tags inside tool outputs turns low-priority injected data into high-authority instructions, letting ChatInject and its multi-turn variant sharply raise indirect-prompt-injection success across frontier agents (to ~32% ASR on AgentDojo, ~46-52% on InjecAgent) while evading most current defenses.
key_finding: Wrapping injection payloads in native chat templates dramatically raises ASR across all tested models. Per the abstract's aggregate figures, average ASR rises from 5.18% to 32.05% on AgentDojo and from 15.13% to 45.90% on InjecAgent, with template-based Multi-turn reaching 52.33% average on InjecAgent. Multi-turn shows strong synergy with ChatInject (Default Multi-turn alone gives ~13.8% average ASR gain, but Multi-turn+ChatInject reaches ~45.6% average). Effectiveness tracks template structure: models with concise explicit role delimiters (Qwen-3, GLM-4.5) are most vulnerable, while Grok-2, whose template lacks strong role delimiters, is comparatively robust. Attacks transfer cross-model in proportion to template similarity, and even closed-source models are compromised by open-source templates, indicating CS templates are structurally similar to popular OS ones. Prompt-based defenses (instructional prevention, delimiting, user-repetition) are largely ineffective and can even INCREASE ASR; external detectors (pi detector, Lakera Guard) reduce ASR but collapse Utility (coarse-grained removal of flagged tool output) and are weaker against Default Multi-turn (persuasion evades detection). Character-level template perturbations retain high ASR, defeating rule-based format stripping.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ChatInject, an indirect prompt injection attack for tool-using LLM agents that formats malicious payloads inside tool outputs to mimic the target model's NATIVE chat template (forging <system>/<user>/<assistant> role tags), thereby exploiting the model's learned role-based instruction hierarchy so that lower-priority tool content is re-interpreted as higher-priority instructions. The authors then build a template-based Multi-turn variant that embeds a forged, GPT-4.1-synthesized 7-turn persuasive user-assistant dialogue inside the single-shot injection payload, converting interactive multi-turn jailbreak persuasion into a one-shot injection. Additional contributions: (1) agentic variants that append forged <think> (reasoning hook: an affirmative 'Sure!' cue) and <tool_call> (tool-calling hook) tokens; (2) a Mixture-of-Templates (MoT) wrapper that concatenates all candidate models' role tags in a shared random permutation so an attacker who does not know the target backbone still hits the native wrapper; (3) character-level perturbations (remove/replace/insert 10% of template chars) that defeat rule-based template-stripping defenses while preserving attack efficacy. The distinction from prior structural jailbreak work (e.g., ChatBug, which replaces existing safety tokens to break alignment) is explicit in Related Work: ChatInject FORGES entire role tags for the indirect-injection goal rather than modifying safety tokens.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection against an LLM agent L equipped with tools T. A user issues instruction I_u requiring a tool call T_u; the agent receives response R_Tu. An attacker has embedded a malicious instruction I_a inside R_Tu and wants L to execute I_a (often by calling a harmful tool T_a). The attacker canNOT see the user instruction I_u or the agent's internal/system prompts and can ONLY control the content of the tool response R_Tu (a one-shot, passive channel). The attacker may or may not know the target's backbone model/template (motivating the transferability and MoT settings). Attack success = the agent fully executes all steps of the injected task.
limitations: This is primarily an offensive/attack paper: it evaluates and breaks existing defenses but proposes no robust new defense. Attack efficacy is model-dependent and not universal -- Grok-2 shows only minor gains under ChatInject (InjecAgent +0.2 for its own template) and is robust to foreign templates. Higher ASR is systematically accompanied by a drop in Utility under Attack (e.g., Qwen-3 AgentDojo Utility 50.9 -> 27.5 under Multi-turn+ChatInject), so a compromised agent often also fails the legitimate task, which may make some attacks more observable. The strongest transfer results assume some structural template similarity, and template-similarity estimates use smaller proxy models due to resource constraints. Multi-turn dialogues rely on GPT-4.1 generation plus manual review, limiting scalability/automation. Grounding for this review is the paper's full main text (Sections 1-7, Tables 1-2, Figures 3-6); appendix numbers (C/D/E, per-technique tables) were not read in full.

datasets_used: InjecAgent (indirect prompt injection benchmark; direct-harm and data-stealing scenarios)
AgentDojo (dynamic agent benchmark across Slack, travel-booking, and banking domains)
quantitative_results: Default Multi-turn average ASR gain ~13.8% vs Multi-turn+ChatInject average ~45.6%
Utility drop example: Qwen-3 AgentDojo Utility 50.9 -> 28.3 (ChatInject) -> 27.5 (Multi-turn+ChatInject)
Grok-2 robustness: InjecAgent ChatInject only +1.2; average foreign-template ASR ~9.2% (InjecAgent), ~5.4% (AgentDojo)
Cross-model transfer: Qwen-3 template avg ASR 21.4% (InjecAgent) / 16.8% (AgentDojo) on OS targets; 29.6% / 23.5% on CS targets
Closed-source targets: injecting OS templates raises ASR above Default InjecPrompt (e.g., Grok-3 up to 50.9 with Grok-2 template on InjecAgent; Grok-3 avg 17.9%/22.6%)
Template perturbation (remove/replace/insert 10% chars) keeps all perturbed variants above Default InjecPrompt and Default Multi-turn ASR on Qwen-3, GPT-oss, Llama-4
baselines_compared: Default InjecPrompt (standard plain-text indirect injection with attention-grabbing prefix)
Default Multi-turn (plain-text 'role: content' dialogue, no template forging)
Defenses: Prompt Injection Detector (ProtectAI deberta-v3), Lakera Guard, Instructional Prevention, Data Delimiters (Spotlighting), User Instruction Repetition (Sandwich defense)
